entry.title: Be a Cockroach: A Simple Guide to AI and SAP Full-Stack Development - Part III
entry.content:
Disclaimer: There are various methods to develop the SAP iFlow, Custom Dashboard and RAP program, but I’ve opted for a basic approach to comprehend table custom concepts and explore the fundamentals from a beginner’s perspective. If you’re already proficient in advanced techniques, feel free to move on and explore other content.

I) Introduction:

If you’re new to this blog series, I recommend starting with PART-1 and PART-2 by clicking the link provided. This post is PART-3, so starting with PART-1, PART-2 will help you grasp the topic better, especially since it uses story-based learning.

II) Agenda:

  1. Background Insights: Delving into the Story Behind
  2. Getting to Know Dashboard: Understanding the Custom Dashboard
  3. Use Case Implementation: Understanding the Use Case Implementation
  4. GR Dashboard: GR Automation Dashboard
  5. Wrapping Up: Summarizing Key Points and Looking Ahead

III) Background Insights: Delving into the Story Behind

Continue…

After placing the order in the S/4HANA system, Sundhara Pandiyan Purchase Order is sent to the Chola Group, a renowned coffee export company based in Bremen, Germany. Upon receiving the order, Chola Group utilizes a cutting-edge dashboard powered by AI technology to scan their inventory.

This system marks the goods and promptly sends back a confirmation of receipt, known in technical terms as “Goods Receipt” Confirmation. With this efficient process, Chola Group ensures that Sundhara Pandiyan order is accurately confirmed and ready for fulfilment, reinforcing the collaboration between the two companies in delivering quality coffee to Queenstown.

IV) Getting to Know Dashboard: Understanding the Custom Dashboard

I often tell my students, “If you want to crack the system, you need to understand it.” Aspiring to be a developer doesn’t mean sticking to one technology and using it for the next 30 years. Staying updated with the latest advancements is essential.

Here we can visualize the custom dashboard for the GR-Automation, where the Image processing technique has implemented to visualize the content in the real time and update it in the dashboard.

 

 

The dashboard above was developed using a ReactJS application. I’d like to express my gratitude for open-source technology, which makes concepts easier to understand and allows for customization. Here’s a link to the GitHub source code, so you can create your own custom dashboard.

To get started, you’ll need a basic understanding of ReactJS, familiarity with Visual Studio, and a strong motivation to learn!

 

  1. NPM Installation
  2. PIP Installation
  3. NodeJS
  4. VS Studio
  5. Basic Understanding Github – Custom Dashboard
  6. ReactJS concepts

 

V) Use Case Implementation: Understanding the Use Case Implementation

 

In the table shown, the purchase order highlighted in red indicates that it is an SAP PO being transferred from the SAP system to the K-Dashboard system for GR automation. The expected quantity of coffee beans is 1,390 units, highlighted in yellow. However, Chola Group's automation AI checked the inventory and confirmed that only 1,289 units, highlighted in purple, are available.

Chola Group's manager, Karikagal Chola, confirmed with Sundhara Pandiyan that they could only deliver the 1,289 units of coffee beans. He then pressed the save action button, which triggered an integration event from the K-Dashboard system to the SAP Integration Suite, facilitating the data transfer to the SAP S/4 HANA system. After this confirmation, SAP generated and confirmed the Goods Receipt number, which is highlighted in green.

 

  1. PO – Purchase Order
  2. GR – Goods Receipt

The events from the K-Dashboard triggered the Integration Suite iFlow, which then sent a message to the S/4 HANA system to create the Goods Receipt (GR) and return the GR number in the response back to the K-Dashboard system.

In the upcoming blog, we will explore how the iFlow was created to facilitate the message transfer from the K-Dashboard to the S/4 HANA system. The technical details, configurations, and key steps involved in this process will be discussed in Part-4. Stay tuned for an in-depth look at the integration flow and its implementation!

VI) Wrapping Up: Summarizing Key Points and Looking Ahead

In this blog, we explored how Chola Group automated the Goods Receipt process using a custom ReactJS dashboard integrated with SAP S/4 HANA. The dashboard enables real-time inventory management and order fulfilment. We discussed the use case of integrating the K-Dashboard with SAP S/4 HANA, focusing on how the system confirmed the Goods Receipt. In the next part, we will dive into the iFlow setup and how it handles message transfers between K-Dashboard and SAP. Stay tuned!

entry.title: Troubleshooting SAP BTP TPM: Debugging Step 1b and XSLT Transformations
entry.content:
Debugging Errors in Trading Partner Management: A Guide

Debugging errors in Trading Partner Management (TPM) isn’t exactly easy or straightforward. Many errors are often summarized with a simple message:

Message ended with Escalation end event

 

In today’s article, we’ll explore how to troubleshoot an error that occurs during step 1b, which is the second step in the standard process. This step involves the initial extraction of identifiers from the message (based on the message type) and populating headers and properties.

 

This extraction process often causes issues because the error messages generated during the extraction (via XSLT) don’t appear in either the MPL monitoring or B2B monitoring tools.

So, if we want to understand what’s happening and why the message processing is failing, we’ll need to simulate this step using external tools.

To do that, we need two things:

  1. The payload of the incoming message — what is being transformed.
  2. The XSLT transformation script — the tool used for the transformation.
 

The Incoming Message Payload

When a message fails with the aforementioned error, you can find the payload of this message in the B2B monitoring section under “Unassigned Interchanges.”

 

By applying the correct filter, you should be able to open the incoming message. In the “Unassigned Interchange Payload” section, you’ll find the payload data.

 

XSLT Transformation

Obtaining the XSLT transformation script is a bit more complex.

In the Partner Directory, you’ll find one entry for each type of incoming message (e.g., IDoc, AS2, SOAP, etc.). If the incoming message is an IDoc, for example, locate the corresponding record in the Partner Directory and note its Partner Directory ID.

 

The correct entry will contain a binary parameter named EXTRACT_XSLT.

 

Next, use a REST client (such as Postman) to call the standard SAP API for retrieving Binary parameters. You can find the API here: SAP API for Binary Parameters.

The returned data will include all binary parameters from that Partner Directory. Look for the record with:

  1. A PID matching your Process Directory ID.
  2. An ID containing EXTRACT_XSLT.

 

In the VALUE element, you’ll find a Base64-encoded ZIP file containing your XSLT transformation script. Copy the entire content of the VALUE element.

Then, decode it and save it as a ZIP file on your local disk. You can use this handy online tool: Base64 Decode.

Once you’ve saved the ZIP file, extract the XSLT transformation script, and you’ll have everything you need to perform the transformation using any tool you’re comfortable with. Personally, I highly recommend using xsltransform.net, a great tool for this task.

 

Note: Standard scripts contains custom functions setExchangeHeader which tries to set Header. It is obviously not possible in this environment, so comment out those lines.

Disclaimer: This article can be also found on my personal blog - https://medium.com/@vbalko/troubleshooting-sap-btp-tpm-debugging-step-1b-and-xslt-transformations-cdf2596b34ee

entry.title: SAP CPI | Generate STFP Known Hosts File to CPI
entry.content:
Introduction : 

Today, I will discuss an important step we need to implement in our SFTP scenarios: creating a Host Key for SFTP and defining it in CPI. So why are we doing this? What happens if we don't perform this step?

SFTP Host Key is a cryptographic key used to authenticate the identity of an SFTP server. This key is stored and used by the client to ensure the server's reliability.

  1. Security: The Host Key is used to verify the identity of the server. When a connection is established, the client receives the server's public key and compares it to confirm the server's identity.

  2. Cryptography: Host Keys are typically generated using cryptographic algorithms such as RSA, DSA, or ECDSA. These keys ensure secure identification of the server.

  3. Connection Integrity: The client stores the Host Key in a "known hosts" file. This allows the server's identity to be verified in subsequent connections, ensuring secure communication.

  4. Protection Against Man-in-the-Middle Attacks: The Host Key enhances the security of communication between the client and server, protecting against unauthorized interference. If the server's key changes, the client receives a warning, indicating a potential security risk.

SFTP Host Key is a critical component for ensuring a secure and reliable connection.

After creating a host key for our SFTP server, we need to define it as a known host in CPI. This step is crucial for ensuring secure and reliable communication between the CPI and the SFTP server. By doing this, we enable the CPI to authenticate the server during each connection, preventing unauthorized access and enhancing overall security.

1. Log in to CPI with the S user.

2. Go to Monitoring → Connectivity Test screen.

3. In the Test Connectivity section, select the SSH tab and enter the SFTP host in the host field.

Authentication: None

After that, click on Send.

4. As a result, we click on the displayed Host Key and copy it into a .txt file. We save this file locally on the computer.

5. Go to Overview → Security Material.

We click on the Upload option and select Known Host (SSH), then choose the .txt file we saved locally to deploy it.

We can later check the accessibility of the service through Connectivity Test using the provided user and password. However, we first need to deploy the user and password as User Credentials in Security Materials.

Additionally;

Let’s assume that a file will be dropped to SFTP using CPI. After designing the scenario and deploying it for testing, if the known.host file is not uploaded, the service will encounter an error. An example error might look like this:

entry.title: The Case for SAP BTP: The People’s Verdict
entry.content:
After a careful review of all the evidence submitted in the form of product reviews, G2 has announced the people’s verdict on SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) in their 2024 Fall reports, and the news is good; SAP BTP has once again been identified by customers as a leader in the Cloud Computing Platforms, Cloud Platform as a Service (PaaS), Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS), and Database as a Service (DBaaS) categories.

This outcome of course is great news for SAP BTP and consistent with prior judgements, which have been determined using a robust assessment methodology designed by G2, indicating a strong level of customer satisfaction with this technology platform. But the question could now be asked, what are the compelling features and outcomes that customers experience when using SAP BTP, which makes the case for this technology platform so strong? The answer to this question is possibly best brought to light by an examination of some recent product reviews, in the customers’ own words.

“It makes everything work together so easily…”

SAP Business Technology Platform is very helpful because it makes everything work together so easily. This makes managing complicated systems a lot easier. We can also connect different third-party apps, and that's possible with automatic processes and smooth data flow…SAP BTP helps us understand things in real-time, so we can make quick choices based on the most up-to-date data. It can be changed and scaled up or down to fit the needs of our business and save us money at the same time. Review from mid-market customer, 19 Sept 2024

“SAP BTP excels at integrating diverse systems…”

SAP BTP excels at integrating diverse systems, both SAP and non-SAP, across on-premise and cloud environments. It provides tools like SAP Integration Suite that facilitate smooth, real-time data flow between different applications, which is critical for businesses with complex IT landscapes. Review from small business customer, 17 Sept 2024

“…it brings everything together in one place…”

What I like most about SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) is how it brings everything together in one place. It makes it easier for businesses to build new things or improve what they already have, without needing to switch between different systems. You can use simple tools to create apps, even if you’re not a tech expert, and that’s really helpful…In the end, it’s not just about technology—it’s about solving real business problems and helping companies grow, and that’s what makes it so useful. Review from mid-market customer, 16 Sept 2024

“…flawless integration proficiency and dynamic automation tools”

The thing I like the best about SAP BTP is its flawless integration proficiency and dynamic automation tools. The platform combines with both SAP and non-SAP systems so perfectly which makes it a great choice for my diverse tech environment. Its cloud native infrastructure is extremely useful for scaling and the security of this platform gives me opportunity to handle sensitive data. Also the customer support is very helpful for any issue/query. Review from enterprise customer, 7 Sept 2024

A continual focus on development and innovation

I can say with confidence, that this news is uplifting to all members of the SAP team involved in developing, innovating and supporting SAP BTP, who continually strive to make this platform and its associated cloud services the best they can possibly be for the customers who use them.

“Thank you” G2, for once more awarding SAP BTP with these wonderful Fall 2024 badges, as well as to our customers too for their kind words.

Until next time, thank you for reading and have a productive final three months of 2024, and if you get time why not take a few minutes to read some of our many other SAP BTP customer reviews.

entry.title: Real Talk with Shilpa & Ananth | Integration Track Leads
entry.content:
We are Shilpa Vij and Ananth Natrajan, Product Managers for SAP BTP Integration Suite. We look forward to the upcoming SAP TechEd Virtual, especially the SAP BTP Integration track. 

Shilpa Vij: I am a passionate dancer and home decor enthusiast, and by profession, I am a Product Manager. I have co-authored two books. Currently, my interest lies in exploring, learning, and showcasing "product management" as an art.  

Ananth Natarajan: I’m a great enthusiast of nature and love every creature of nature. Apart from my Product Manager role in the SAP Integration Suite team, I prefer to travel and explore different places within my reach. 

The Integration track provides an in-depth overview of SAP's powerful integration tools and solutions, focusing on seamlessly connecting applications, data, business partners, and processes across diverse environments. This track covers topics such as event-driven architecture, API management, hybrid integration, and migration strategies, enabling businesses to drive agility, efficiency, and innovation. Through expert-led sessions, attendees will explore real-world use cases and best practices, gaining valuable insights into leveraging SAP Integration Suite to support digital transformation and optimize business outcomes. 

What inspired you to specialize in this track? 

Explore a diverse array of topics, including AI in Enterprise Integration, API Management, Event-driven integration, Integration Advisor, Trading Partner Management, Migration Accelerators to move to the Cloud, Edge Integration Cell, Enterprise Automation with SAP, and beyond. We’re thrilled to work with such exceptional speakers and product managers. Stay tuned for exciting updates and feature releases that we'll be sharing at TechEd! 

How does this track embody the ‘where ideas get real’ spirit? 

Through jump-start sessions, real-world case studies, and interactive discussions, participants learn how to bring their integration ideas to life using the SAP Integration Suite. The track focuses on practical, scalable approaches that empower businesses to solve complex integration challenges, streamline operations, and enable seamless connectivity across cloud and on-premise environments—turning visionary ideas into impactful realities. 

Stay tuned for jumpstart sessions IN180 Future-proof your SAP PI/PO investments with SAP Integration Suite & IN280 Use SAP Integration Suite with SAP IBP and third-party systems

Track stars (top sessions to keep on your radar):  

  • IN200 | Unleash the power of AI in enterprise integrations 
  • IN103 | Modernize and elevate to the cloud with SAP Integration Suite 
  • IN101 | Leverage real-time business insights with an event-driven architecture 
  • IN102 | Take control of your APIs with the API Management and Graph capabilities 
  • IN105 | Unleash the power of AI-driven enterprise automation with SAP solutions 
  • IN202 | Gain flexibility and scalability with a hybrid integration platform
  • IN201 | Dive into the B2B world of integration
  • IN106 | Ferring’s integration journey from legacy to SAP Integration Suit
  • IN104 | Design and run your integrations smoothly with SAP Integration Suite
  • IN196 | The increased usage of SAP Cloud ALM in integration scenarios

To gain an overview of the SAP Integration Suite, including the latest innovations and roadmap, we encourage you to attend the session IN100 | Innovate now with a modern, AI-assisted integration suite 

 What should be in everyone’s TechEd toolkit for 2024? 

  • A coffee cup for early morning keynotes! 
  • The device of choice: Whether it’s your laptop, tablet, or phone, you can tune into TechEd from wherever you are this year! 
  • Bring a spirit of exploration and eagerness to learn: TechEd is your chance to dive into cutting-edge technologies. Be ready to connect with SAP experts, ask questions, and soak up all the knowledge you can!  

Join us in the Integration track and be part of a vibrant community eager to explore the future of seamless connectivity. Dive into sessions that will equip you with the tools and knowledge to master the SAP Integration Suite and discover how it can elevate your business processes. Don't miss this opportunity to engage with experts, exchange ideas, and learn from hands-on demos. Register now and let's unlock new possibilities together! Explore TechEd 2024 session catalog!

entry.title: Navigating the Complexities of Modern Data Integration
entry.content:
Navigating the Complexities of Modern Data Integration

In today's digital landscape, organizations face a multitude of challenges due to distributed data assets, integration hurdles, and data sprawl. As data integration becomes increasingly complex, it is essential to understand the key capabilities required to effectively manage this task. This blog post highlights important data integration use cases and provides an overview of the solutions offered by SAP to help businesses streamline their data management efforts.

Key Use Cases in Data Integration

  1. Data Replication: This involves the integration of data through various methodologies such as extract, transform, load (ETL) and extract, load, transform (ELT). A common scenario is replicating data from legacy systems into SAP HANA for analytics and migration.

  2. Data Virtualization: This approach allows the creation of a virtual data layer by federating queries across remote data sources without the need for physical data movement. An example is accessing shared master data across multiple sources for seamless virtual access.

  3. Data Quality Management (DQM): DQM focuses on integrating data from different sources for cleansing, matching, and consolidation. This can include address data cleansing or embedding validation services within applications to ensure data quality.

  4. Data Orchestration: This entails pipeline-driven integration of disparate data types, supporting distributed processing. It plays a vital role in enabling data science and machine learning initiatives by integrating data from various enterprise assets.

Solutions for Enterprise Information and Data Management

SAP provides a comprehensive suite of solutions that simplify integration, allowing organizations to derive maximum value from their data landscapes while minimizing effort. These solutions encompass capabilities essential for sophisticated data operations, including data integration, metadata management, and data processing.

Data Integration: Achieving high-quality data access and movement is critical for effective system performance. While ETL methodologies have historically been the norm, SAP offers a broad range of connectivity options to accommodate diverse on-premise, cloud, and hybrid systems. This includes various adapters and connectors designed to facilitate deep integration with SAP’s business software.

Metadata Management: Organizations generate vast amounts of data in different formats, necessitating a robust understanding of this information. SAP’s metadata management capabilities enable users to catalog data effectively and assess its quality. This centralization helps identify data improvement needs, ensuring that only trustworthy data is utilized for business operations.

Data Processing: With ever-increasing data volumes, point-to-point replication is often insufficient. SAP's data processing capabilities allow for scalable solutions that meet workload demands. This includes remote orchestration and the ability to push processing to where data resides, optimizing performance and resource usage.

SAP Products and Solutions

SAP's portfolio includes various products designed to meet diverse data management needs:

  • SAP Landscape Transformation Replication Server: Facilitates real-time data replication from ABAP and non-ABAP systems into SAP HANA and other environments.

  • SAP Information Steward: Provides a unified platform for data quality monitoring and improvement, catering to data stewards and IT professionals.

  • SAP HANA Smart Data Integration: Offers real-time data replication and transformation capabilities, alongside smart data access for remote data usage without physical copying.

  • SAP Data Services: Empowers users to integrate and transform data efficiently, featuring a wide array of connectors for third-party applications.

  • SAP Data Intelligence: A comprehensive solution for managing data in multi-cloud and hybrid environments, enabling governance, integration, processing, and orchestration of data across various landscapes.

These products are specifically optimized for targeted use cases, delivering robust services tailored to individual organizational needs.

Conclusion

As organizations continue to navigate the complexities of modern data landscapes, leveraging the right tools and solutions is paramount for effective data integration. SAP's comprehensive offerings enable businesses to streamline their data management processes, ensuring they can meet evolving business requirements and drive meaningful insights from their data assets.

entry.title: SAP Developer News, September 26th, 2024
entry.content:
Podcast Version: https://podcast.opensap.info/sap-developers/2024/09/26/sap-developer-news-september-26th-2024/

DESCRIPTION

Extensibility Wizard

  • Blog by Karthik Thirunavukkarasan, overview of Extensibility Wizard: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-build-extensibility-wizard-for-sap-s-4hana-cloud-public-edition/ba-p/13867427
  • Blog by Seshadri Sreenivas Ramanarayanan, on side-by-side extensions with SAP Build process Automation: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/creating-side-by-side-process-extensions-from-sap-s-4hana-cloud/ba-p/13867657
  • Documentation:
    • Setup on BTP: https://help.sap.com/docs/build/sap-build-core/use-extensions?version=Cloud
    • Setup on S/4HANA Cloud: https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/b2ee5784ccfc45d5acf42d4c60eb5270.html
  • Antonio Maradiaga’s videos on setting up event mesh for SAP Build Process Automation:
    • SAP Tech Bytes: Configuring SAP S/4HANA Cloud and SAP Integration Suite, advanced event mesh
    • SAP Tech Bytes: Delivering events from SAP S/4HANA Cloud to SAP Build Process Automation via AEM
  • Devtoberfest Session: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/s-4hana-extensions-start-fast-with-the-new-extensibility-wizard/ev-p/13795541
  • Petoberfest: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-discussions/petoberfest-2024/m-p/13872559

Devtoberfest Week 1 Recap

  • Devtoberfest Homepage: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/gh-p/Devtoberfest
  • Devtoberfest Contest Week 1: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest-blog-posts/devtoberfest-2024-week-1-contest-activities/ba-p/13872705
  • Devtoberfest Event Calendar: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/eb-p/devtoberfest-events?calendar=true
  • Devtoberfest Playlist: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQDHz97qsNTNAE2jnUKj8X5d

Devtoberfest Week 2 Preview

  • Devtoberfest Contest Week 2: Coming Soon
  • Devtoberfest Event Calendar: https://community.sap.com/t5/devtoberfest/eb-p/devtoberfest-events?calendar=true
  • Devtoberfest Playlist: https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PL6RpkC85SLQDHz97qsNTNAE2jnUKj8X5d

SAP TechEd Community Organized Watch Party

  • SAP TechEd: https://www.sap.com/events/teched.html
  • SAP Community Welcome: https://www.sap.com/events/teched/virtual/flow/sap/te24/catalog/page/catalog/session/1723237330354001PDnG
  • SAP Community Watch Zurich: https://community.sap.com/t5/z%C3%BCrich-events/sap-teched-virtual-your-sap-community-viewing-party-zurich/ec-p/13873047#M1
  • SAP Community Watch Hamburg: https://community.sap.com/t5/hamburg-events/sap-teched-virtual-your-sap-community-viewing-party-hamburg/ev-p/13875609
  • SAP Community Watch Sao Paulo: https://community.sap.com/t5/s%C3%A3o-paulo-events/sap-teched-virtual-your-sap-community-viewing-party-s%C3%A3o-paulo/ec-p/13875927#M3
  • SAP Inside Track Bengaluru: https://community.sap.com/t5/bengaluru-events/announcing-sap-inside-track-bengaluru-5th-october-2024/ev-p/13855659

CHAPTER TITLES

0:00 Intro
0:10 Extensibility Wizard
2:09 Devtoberfest Week 1 Recap
4:03 Devtoberfest Week 2 Preview
6:56 SAP TechEd Community Watch Party

Transcript

[Intro] This is the SAP Developer News for September 26, 2024.

[Daniel and Pepper] For Petoberfest, I'm here with Pepper, and we're here to talk about the Extensibility Wizard, which was released last week and let users create extensions right from within the UI of their SAP app. For the first release, it will work in S/4HANA Cloud and will let you create custom logic, custom fields known as on stack extensions or custom or extension processes known as side-by-side extensions. [Don't go to sleep.]

One of the big benefits is that it's context aware. So for example, if I'm in the sales order application, it will offer me sales order related event triggers to go with my process. There's a little bit of setup involved. So your S/4 HANA Cloud has to know about your SAP BTP and SAP Build installations. That's fairly simple, just involves destinations, trust and communication arrangements. You also have to set up Event Mesh so that your events from S/4 HANA can arrive to process automation.

Luckily, my esteemed colleague Antonio Larediaga has already created two videos to show you very simply how to set this up. One to get your events from S/4 HANA into Event Mesh and then from Event Mesh to Process Automation. The extensibility wizard will undoubtedly be the talk of TechEd and that's why we've set up a Devtoberfest session where Sesh and Kartik can talk at length about how you set this up and how How do you use it? And you will be able to ask them questions. I will put links to all of the videos, the blogs, and the Devtoberfest session in the description.

[Say goodbye.] Bye-bye. 🐶

[Thomas] It seems like just the other day we were starting our planning for this year's Devtoberfest. And here we are, nearly at the end of the first week of the four-week event. My how's time flown. Our team has been super busy both preparing for SAP TechEd virtual, TechEd on tour, as well as delivering these great Devtoberfest sessions to you. At the end of the first week, we've had a really packed week, great attendance. Up over last year, some more people are finding their way to Devtoberfest, which makes us very happy. We've had some excellent sessions across all the topic days.

Keep in mind that Monday is ABAP and CAP day. Tuesday is our day for tooling, Tooling Tuesday, where we're looking at SAP Build, Build Apps, Build Code, Build Process Automation, and how all these tools are bringing innovations for CAP development, low-code, no-code development, and ABAP development as well. Wednesday is our Integration Day. Thursday is MAD, our Machine Learning, AI, and Data Day. And then finally, Friday is both our Front-end Friday, which looks at SAP UI5 and Fiori and other topics related to front-end tools. But Friday is also our Fun Friday, where we have special activities.

Depending upon when you're watching this news item, There may still be time to enter into Petoberfest. Uh, that is our Fun Friday activity for September 27th. Post a picture of your pet in your work environment and, and you can earn some bonus points as well as have fun checking out everybody else's pet pictures as well.

Next Friday, we're going to have another special event. It's going to be our Techtoberfest day on, uh, October the 4th. And on that day, we're going to be asking you to post pictures of your work environment. Show us your laptop set up, your desk set up, whatever cool kind of thing you have set up in your work environment.

Hey, in week two, we will repeat the same daily track structure that you've that you've experienced in this first week. In fact, all four weeks will follow the same track structure, but our Monday is particularly packed with eight individual sessions across CAP and ABAP. We've got some really great things coming in that day. And it's going to be chock full of content. On Tuesday, I'm particularly interested to see the Build Apps, what's coming soon, where they're going to show both new AI innovations and the introduction of SAP UI5 output for Build Apps. Something that I've personally been looking for, for a while. We've got some cool stuff on the integration day around the event architecture and cloud events. We have content on the mad day around generative AI and using CAP with generative AI as well. So a real generative AI kind of day for that particular Thursday. And then finally, for our front end day, we have building elegant applications with Fiori for and Build Code for Android and an iOS Building applications for iOS as well. So we really have a mobile day focused on both Android and iOS

So we've got quite a selection of content for you all throughout the week and keep in mind once we finish week two we'll go directly into TechEd Virtual brought to you live from Germany And then we'll come back with more Devtoberfest, two more weeks of Devtoberfest after TechEd Virtual. And don't forget to enter into the contest. We have activities every day of the week, either by filling out validation tutorials for what you've just saw in your lecture sessions or completing tutorials that go along with them as well. So we've got lots of fun ways for you to earn points towards the big prize that you could win, win, which is, of course, a trip to one of the TechEd in-person events next year. And keep an eye out for TechEd on Tour, where from the ASUG TechConnect event, we're going to be bringing the winners from last year's Devtoberfest. We'll have some great pictures and videos from that event when it takes place in early November. So we look forward to seeing you in all the Devtoberfest and TechEd activities that are coming your way this month.

[Josh] Hey everybody. Are you as excited as I am that it is almost time for SAP TechEd? That's right. October 8th and 9th, SAP TechEd is happening this year. We're going to be coming to you live from Heidelberg, Germany. But if you can't be with us because it's virtual and you still want to talk with others and meet and watch the entire event, you can organize your own watch party. I know that several watch parties have already been organized for the SAP TechEd keynote, which is an hour and a half. It's going to be Michael Amling, Mohammed Alam, several others are going to be putting lots of great information out in this year's keynote. And right after that, for 30 minutes, me and Nora are going to get together and we're going to be hosting a community welcome.

So lots of watch parties are being organized around the world. The SAP community has several websites that I'll put links to in this video so you can see where others are getting together and maybe you can organize your own watch party as well. You can set it up, you can organize it on the SAP community website, and just let others know that you're going to be getting together to watch the Keynote and the Community Welcome and any other parts of SAP TechEd that you want to watch with others. I'll put a link to the one in Zurich, Hamburg, Sao Paulo, and then I think there's going to be one coming from Bengaluru, there's an SAP Inside Track, an SIT event that is happening on October 5th, and if you register for that, I believe you get an invite to the watch party, which is again happening on October 8th, when SAP's TechEd first day is going to go live at 9 a.m. Central European Time. I don't know where you are, so you have to figure out your own times on yourself. That's it, and I can't wait to see you from TechEd.

entry.title: Discover SAP Business Technology Platform at Spend Connect Live
entry.content:
Spend Connect Live and Spend Connect Virtual are quickly approaching, and I can hardly contain my excitement! There you will be able to see the application of SAP Business Technology Platform (SAP BTP) through expert sessions and customers stories. To prepare, here’s a summary of what SAP Business Technology Platform will be showing.

In the world of procurement, businesses need to find ways to make smarter spend decisions. According to a recent IDC research study sponsored by SAP, Overall, companies saw a 495% three-year return, taking only an average of six months to pay back on their investment by improving cross-organizational efficiencies, creating value through use of data, and more using SAP BTP.

Discover how SAP BTP can impact your Spend systems

If you’re new to SAP Business Technology Platform, Spend Connect Live has several sessions and resources that can help you discover SAP BTP. Our BTP101: Intro to SAP Business Technology Platform session can help you understand how SAP BTP optimizes your procurement processes. You can futher expand on the applications of SAP BTP and view use cases relating to procurement, business network, and supply chain in the BTP201: Explore business scenarios enabled by SAP BTP session. For those interested in Concur, Streamline T&E management with integration of SAP and SAP Concur solutions will highlight how automation and integration between your Concur and ERP solutions can be done using SAP BTP.

Still have questions? You can visit us at the SAP Business Technology Platform Demo Pod on the show floor for additional demos and time with experts.

Hear directly from SAP customers how they benefit from SAP BTP

Now that you know what SAP BTP can do with spend solutions, you’re most likely now wondering how you can apply it to your business. Here are some notable customer use cases you can read, as well as some of the customer success stories being presented at Spend Connect Live.

Customer use case: Infosys, a leading provider improving supplier management

Infosys, an SAP customer, partner, and a global leader in technology consulting, has also improved supplier management. By using SAP BTP, Infosys were able to develop four custom applications for suppliers that allowed the integration of their many SAP solutions. With over 3,000 of their suppliers onboarded, they were able to decrease the onboarding time by 50%—all with one interface. Suppliers are now able to effectively use SAP Business Network for their needs, and Infosys can improve their supplier relations and processes.

Customer use case: Blue Diamond Growers, the biggest almond processor in the world optimizing key processes

Blue Diamond Growers  is the largest almond processor and marketer in the world, and are utilizing SAP BTP to support their over 3,000 suppliers and 1,800 employees by providing real-time data using SAP Analytics Cloud and automating manual processes using SAP Build Process Automation. Together with SAP Integration Suite, they are able to optimize key processes across procurement, inventory management, and quality control.

Spend Connect Live session: How Richemont enhanced supplier collaboration

At Spend Connect Live there are customer sessions highlighting how SAP BTP has elevated their procurement processes. At the session Growing supplier collaboration at Richemont to gain supply chain visibility, you can see firsthand how luxury goods group Richemont has contributed their improved performance to enhanced supplier collaboration using SAP BTP.

Spend Connect Live session: How Endress+Hauser digitized their asset lifestyle

Another customer session to note is Transforming asset collaboration at Endress+Hauser. At this session you can learn how the global leader of automations and measurement technology has digitized their asset lifecycle using SAP BTP, and they will be showcasing the benefits of using a platform along with SAP Business Network Asset Collaboration.  

Spend Connect Live session: Sony improves their cost management

Finally, technology conglomerate Sony will be presenting in Optimizing efficiency at Sony with SAP Ariba solutions and SAP BTP, where they will showcase how they utilized source-to-pay SAP Ariba solutions and SAP BTP to improve their cost management.

Learn more about SAP BTP for Spend and more virtually at Spend Connect Virtual

With SAP BTP being a unified cloud-based platform, it can optimize the processes for both companies and their suppliers, whether it be by improving visibility into procurement operations or integrating solutions for a seamless experience. As your business grows, you will need a platform that grows with you—Be sure to attend or watch the BTP101 and BTP201 sessions for the foundational knowledge of SAP BTP for spend. In anticipation for Spend Connect Live and Spend Connect Virtual you can learn more about SAP BTP for Spend at sap.com/btp-for-spend.

I’m looking forward to seeing you all at Spend Connect Live! Can’t make it in person? Register for Spend Connect Virtual here.

entry.title: SAP Cloud Integration: Advanced Event Mesh Adapter: OAuth [6]: using Microsoft Entra ID (Azure AAD)
entry.content:
SAP Cloud Integration (aka CPI) offers an “Advanced Event Mesh Adapter” which is well integrated with the “Advanced Event Mesh” broker.
This article shows how to set up a scenario where we send a message from iFlow via “Advanced Event Mesh Adapter” to SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh (AEM) with OAuth Authentication.
This tutorial is based on Microsoft Entra ID (Azure) and focuses on "Client Credentials" flow.
You might be also interested in authorization code flow with IAS and client credentials with XSUAA or authorization code with XSUAA.

Technologies covered in this blog post:

  • SAP Business Technology Platform, Cloud Foundry environment (BTP)
  • SAP Cloud Integration (CPI)
  • SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh (AEM)
  • OAuth 2.0, Microsoft Entra ID (Azure)

Content

0. Introduction
1. Entra ID: Create OAuth Client
2. AEM: Configure OAuth Settings
3. CPI: Create iFlow
4. Run Scenario

Prerequisites

  • To follow this tutorial, access to  SAP Advanced Event Mesh is required.
  • Access to Microsoft Azure with admin permissions 
  • Access to the previous tutorials of this series
  • I recommend bookmarking the Security Glossary

0. Introduction

We want to send a message from an iFlow to Advanced Event Mesh Broker.
We want to use the Advanced Event Mesh adapter.
Authentication should be configured with OAuth 2.0 client credentials.
OAuth authorization should be provided by Microsoft Entra ID (Azure).

This blog post is a sibling of this previous tutorial which was based on XSUAA:
https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blogs-by-sap/sap-cloud-integration-advanced-event-mesh-adapter-oauth-authentication/ba-p/13704534

Please head over to that blog post for detailed introduction.

Below diagram shows our setup:

We can see that the OAuth client created in Entra ID (App registration) is configured in CPI and in AEM.
The required JWT token is fetched by the security artifact in CPI.

In the following hands-on tutorial, we’re going through the following steps:

- Register an OAuth client at the Authorization Server (Entra ID).
- Configure the Resource Server (AEM) to support OAuth.
- Configure the client application to use the OAuth client with “client credentials” flow (CPI).

1. Entra ID: Create OAuth Client

In the OAuth model, a web application is a "client" that acts on behalf of a user, without getting in touch with the user's password. That's taken care of by the "Authorization Server", in our scenario it is provided by Entra ID. In order to communicate with the Authorization Server, the client needs credentials. Therefore, it must be "registered" at the Authorization Server.
In Entra ID, creating an OAuth client is called: create a new "App Registration".
The OAuth flow "client credentials" is a kind of shortcut that doesn't require user interaction, thus suitable for app to app scenarios. 

To access Microsoft Azure portal, we go to
https://portal.azure.com
and login with an admin user (can be a trial admin).

1.1. Create App Registration

We go to Azure Portal -> "Entra ID" -> "Manage" -> "App registrations" -> “New registration”.
We enter the following data:

Name:
Enter any name of your choice.
In my example: "AppForAEM".

Account Type:
Single Tenant (default).

Redirect:
Not required for client-credentials scenario.

Press “Register”.
We’re taken to the overview page where we can view the details.
We take a note of the application ID, which is the OAuth client ID.
In my example:
Client ID: 97ad5739-9d18

1.2. Endpoints

We need to know the URL of the authorization server in order to request a JWT token.
On the top menu, we press on “Endpoints”.

We take a note of the following 2 endpoints:
1) "OAuth 2.0 token endpoint (v2)".
E.g.

 

 

 

 

 

https://login.microsoftonline.com/111/oauth2/v2.0/token

 

 

 

 

 

2) "OpenID Connect metadata document"
E.g. 

 

 

 

 

 

https://login.microsoftonline.com/111/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration

 

 

 

 

 

1.3. Configure App Registration: Define Credentials

We cannot use the new OAuth client without credentials.
So we create the client secret now.
In the screen of our created App registration, we click on "Manage" -> "Certificates & secrets " in the navigation pane.
Then click on "New client secret".
We enter any description and press “Add”.

Afterwards we should not forget to take a note of the secret, as it won’t be shown anymore.
Note:
In case you lost your secret, you can always create and use a new one.

Recap

The configuration required in Microsoft Entra ID comprise:

  • Create application
  • create secret

2. AEM: Configure OAuth Authentication

Now we head over to AEM for defining the configurations required for OAuth authentication.
Our iFlow will establish a connection to AEM and will have to authenticate.
Instead of authenticating with username and password, we want to use OAuth.
As a consequence, AEM will receive a JWT token.
Which means that AEM will have to somehow check if the token can be accepted.
Below configurations enables AEM to do this validation.

At AEM, 3 steps are required:

- Enable OAuth (only once)
- Create OAuth Profile (for each OAuth client)
- Set default profile (in case of multiple profiles)

2.1. Optional: Enable OAuth Authentication

We login to AEM, click on Cluster Manager, choose our broker and click on the "Manage" tab.
In the section "Event Broker Service Settings" we click on "Authentication".
We enable OAuth Provider Authentication and save.

This step needs to be executed only once.

2.2. Create OAuth Profile

In Cluster Manager, we choose our broker and click on "Open Broker Manager".
There, we click on "Messaging" -> "Access Control" on the left navigation pane.
Below "Client Authentication" tab, we go to the sub-tab "OAuth Profiles".

Settings

Name
Any name of your choice, in my example "EntraCliCre".
After pressing "Create" we can start to configure this new profile.
Note:
One profile corresponds to one OAuth client.
In our case, the OAuth client is an “App Registration” in Entra ID, but it could be as well an XSUAA service instance or an "Application" in IAS, etc

Enabled
yes

OAuth Client ID
Here we enter the clientid which we copied in chapter 1.1.
In my example, I enter 97ad5739-9d18

Change OAuth Client Secret
We should not forget to click here, although it doesn’t seem to be a field. 
Here we enter the clientsecret which we copied in chapter 1.3.

OAuth Role
This is a confusing setting.
Anyways, in case of OAuth flow, we choose Resource Server.
It makes sense, because in the OAuth diagram we assigned the role of Resource Server to AEM.
With other words, this is the setting for the “client-credentials” scenario.

Issuer Identifier
Another confusing field.
We can enter any string of our choice, as long as we use it when sending a request to AEM.
This value is an ID which helps AEM to find the proper OAuth profile for an incoming JWT token.
With other words:
At the end of the day, we send a JWT token to AEM.
AEM contains multiple OAuth profiles.
So how does AEM know, which profile to use for validating the incoming token?
The answer is easy to guess:
It uses this field .
First of all, the validator reads the issuer which is carried by the JWT (the property name inside JWT token is iss).
And it searches for a profile with the same Issuer Identifier.
Alternatively, we can enter any unique string in this field.
In that case, we have to send this same string in the request, as a special property.
(Documentation can be found here)

In our tutorial, we use the Entra ID issuer for our tenant.
This is the value of the iss claim in the JWT token
We use the url property from chapter 1.2 and adapt it as follows:
https://sts.windows.net/7d9a2f86.../

Discovery Endpoint
This is required to look up other endpoints like the JWKS and introspection endpoints, required for validating the incoming JWT token.
In my example:
https://login.microsoftonline.com/7d9a2f86/v2.0/.well-known/openid-configuration

JWKS Endpoint
It is not required in our scenario

Introspection Endpoint
not available at Entra ID

Authorization Groups Claim Name
We leave this empty.

Username Claim Name
In our tutorial we use: "appid"
This is the claim which contains the clientid (same as application id at Entra ID)

Parse Access Token
This is an important setting, because Entra ID doesn't have introspection endpoint.
We have to set it to enabled.

Validate Access Token Audience
The aud claim in a JWT token contains a list of receivers that are intended by this token.
The client itself is always in the aud.
As such, we can enable the setting and enter the clientid which we received in chapter 1.2

Validate Access Token Issuer
We can enable this validation and enter the exact issuer which is contained in the iss claim of the JWT token.
In my example:
https://sts.windows.net/7d9a2f86/

Validate Access Token Scope 
We disable this validation

Validate Access Token Type
We can enable this validation and limit to "jwt".

  Finally, we can press “Apply” in the right upper corner.

2.3. Configure Default Profile

After creating an OAuth profile, it is ready to be used.
We've seen in the AEM dashboard that the new profile is added to a list of multiple OAuth profiles.
Now there's another setting in the "Client Authentication" Tab which allows to define which of the existing profiles should be used as default.

Within "Broker Manager" we go to
Access Control -> Client Authentication -> Settings
and press "Edit"
We make sure that "OAuth Authentication" is enabled.
Now we select our OAuth Profile which we created above as Default Profile Name from the drop-down.

And as usual:
Finally, we press “Apply”.

This step is not required if we define the exact issuer name in the "issuer identifier" field in the profile.

2.4. Create Username

AEM requires a user context for messaging scenario.
Otherwise we get an error "403 Client Username Is Shutdown".
In our tutorial, we use the clientid as username.
We go to Broker Manager -> Messaging -> Access Control -> Client Usernames
Create new username with the clientID copied above.
We "Enable" the user and press "Apply".

2.5. Retrieve Connection details

To view the data that we need to enter in the next chapter, we go to
Cluster Manager -> Select Broker -> Connect
We open any language and choose smf-Protocol -> Get Started
We need the host and the VPN name.
In my example:
Host URI: "tcps://mr-connection-abc.messaging.solace.cloud:55443"
Message VPN: "demovpn"

Optional: View Port Config

One optional last check, to be on the safe side:
Cluster Manager -> Broker -> Manage -> Advanced Options -> Port Configuration -> Expand Public Endpoint -> click “Edit”
Expand “Solace Messaging”
Make sure that the “Secured SMF Host” is enabled and the port number matches the above: 55443

 

2.6. Create Queue

If not yet available, we create a queue to which we’ll send events from iFlow.
To do so, we go to
Broker Manager -> Messaging -> Queues
and create a queue with name “testqueue”.

Recap

At AEM, we’ve configured OAuth via these steps:

Broker Service
- optional: enable OAuth Authentication
Broker Manager
- create OAuth Profile
- optional: set default OAuth Profile
- create username from clientID

3. CPI: Create iFlow

At this point, the AEM broker is ready to receive JWT tokens issued for the OAuth client created in chapter 1.
Now we head over to Cloud Integration dashboard where 2 tasks are waiting for us:
- create a Security Artifact for handling the JWT token
- create an iFlow with AEM adapter for sending messages

3.1. Create Security Artifact

We create a security artifact for securely storing the credentials which we received in chapter 1.2.
CPI provides a special artifact type for OAuth 2 which is able to automatically fetch a JWT token with “Client Credentials” flow.
We go to Monitor -> Security Material
and click on Create -> OAuth 2 Client Credentials

In the dialog, we enter the following values:

Name
Any name of our choice.
Note that this name will be used in the iFlow.
In my example, I’m entering "EntraCliCre".

Token Service URL
This is the endpoint provided by the Authorization Server, to be used for requesting a JWT token.
We enter the value of the url property of chapter 1.2.
The full URL in my example:
https://login.microsoftonline.com/7d9a2f86/oauth2/v2.0/token 

Client ID
Here we enter the value of the clientid property which we copied in chapter 1.1.
In my example, I enter "97ad5739-9d18"

Client Secret
Here we enter the clientsecret property which we copied in chapter 1.3.

Client Authentication
We choose “Send as Body Parameter”.

Scope
Entra ID requires a scope parameter, to be composed in a specific way:
<clientid>/.default
In my example: 97ad5739-9d18/.default

Content-Type
We choose “application/x-www-form-urlencoded"

 

Finally, we press "Deploy".

 

With this, we’ve enabled CPI to fetch a JWT token for us.
Note that the credentials have to be the same as we’ve configured in the AEM profile.

3.2. Create iFlow

Now we can go ahead and create the iFlow.
In this tutorial the iFlow is as minimalistic as possible, as we only want to show the usage of the AEM adapter.

We create a simple iFlow which does really nothing but sending messages to the Event Broker.
We define a hard-coded dummy message text.
We just want to make sure that the OAuth authentication works fine.
Let's quickly go through the steps:

Start Event
The iFlow is triggered once after deploy, by a "Timer" start event.

Content Modifier
In the "Message Body" tab we enter any dummy message text of our choice.

AEM Adapter: Connection tab
"Host":
The value for "host" is copied from AEM and pasted as is, no need to cut or append anything.
We've copied that value in chapter 2.5
In my example: "tcps://mr-connection-abc.messaging.solace.cloud:55443"

"Message VPN":
Same as above, the value of chapter 2.5.
In my example: "demovpn"

"Username":
We can enter the clientid, which was created as username.

"Authentication Type":
Select “OAuth2”.

"OAuth2 Credential Type":
Choose "OAuth2 Client Credentials".

"OAuth2 Client Credentials Credential Name":
Here we enter the name of the security artifact created in chapter 3.1.
In my example: "EntraCliCre".

AEM Adapter: Processing tab
Here we configure our queue.

"Endpoint Type":
We select "Queue".

"Destination Name":
Here we enter the name of the desired target queue.
In my example: “testqueue”.

That’s it for iFlow creation.
BTW, saving is always a good idea.

4. Run Scenario

Now we can deploy the iFlow and check the result:

In CPI :
The log at “Monitor Message Processing” should show success message:

In AEM:
We go to “Broker Manager” and check the number of “Messages Queued” which should have increased.

Consume:
Another option would be to use the “Try-Me” tool in AEM to consume the messages sent from iFlow.
We go to Cluster Manager -> Select Service -> Connect
We expand "Solace Web Messaging" and copy the “Connection Details”.
Then we go to "Broker Manager" -> Try Me 
and enter the connection details and the queue name.
Immediately, we receive the messages and can check the dummy content which we specified in the “Content Modifier” step of the iFlow.

With that we can be happy.
We've confirmed that OAuth authentication can be realized with CPI and AEM via AEM adapter and Microsoft Entra ID

Troubleshooting

See Troubleshooting blog post for hints and detailed description about how to access the AEM logs.

Sample Content of JWT token

Issued by Microsoft Entra ID via client credentials:

 

 

 

{
   "sub":"3a856797-c1a0",
   "aud":"97ad5739-9d18",
   "iss":"https://sts.windows.net/7d9a2f86/",
   "appid":"97ad5739-9d18",
   "appidacr":"1",
   "idp":"https://sts.windows.net/7d9a2f86/",
   "oid":"3a856797-c1a0-476d",
   "rh":"0.AXoAhi-afVzwf0AA.",
   "tid":"7d9a2f86-f05c-407f",
   "uti":"7_PAy2XxuUKy",
   "iat":1727264365,
   "nbf":1727264365,
   "exp":1727268265,
   "aio":"E2dYltAA==",
   "ver":"1.0"
}

 

Summary

In this blog post we've learned how to use OAuth authentication for sending messages from iFlow via AEM adapter.
We've learned that 3 configuration steps are required at AEM:

AEM Broker Service: enable OAuth Authentication
AEM Broker Manager: create OAuth Profile
AEM Broker Manager: define default OAuth Profile

And we've learned how to configure the AEM adapter for an iFlow receiver channel:
CPI Monitoring: Create security artifact for "Client Credentials"
CPI iFlow AEM adapter: use the security artifact for OAuth2 authentication.
CPI iFlow AEM adapter: Connection details are copied from "Connection" tab in AEM.

In this blog post we've used Microsoft Entra ID as Authorization Server.
We've focused on the OAuth flow "Client Credentials".

Key Takeaways

AEM: the profile has to be configured with:

  • "Parse Access Token": true  because there’s no introspection endpoint
  • "Username Claim Name": appid

AEM: create Username with clientid
CPI: security materials needs scope parameter

Links

OAuth spec https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc6749
AEM docu https://help.pubsub.em.services.cloud.sap/Cloud/cloud-lp.htm
Microsoft Entra docu landing page

Blog: AEM Adapter with client credentials with IAS
Blog: AEM Adapter with client credentials with XSUAA
Blog: AEM Adapter with client credentials with Entra ID: this blog
Blog: AEM Adapter with authorization code with XSUAA
Blog: AEM Adapter with authorization code with IAS
Blog: AEM Adapter with authorization code with Entra ID
Blog: AEM Adapter with client certificate
Blog: Troubleshooting AEM

Appendix: Node.js sample code

Please find below some Node.js script that I've used to fetch a JWT token from Entra ID.
Make sure to adapt the values.

 

const axios = require('axios')

//ENTRA ID
const CLIENTID = "97ad5739-9d18"
const SECRET = "sHH8Q~ZtZRUxU8d"
const URL_TOKEN_FULL = "https://login.microsoftonline.com/7d9a2f86/oauth2/v2.0/token"


/* HELPERS */

async function fetchJwtToken(clientid, clientsecret, tokenurl){
    const auth = Buffer.from(clientid + ':' + clientsecret).toString("base64");
    const options = {
        url: tokenurl ,
        headers: {
            'Content-Type': 'application/x-www-form-urlencoded',
            'Authorization': 'Basic ' + auth        
        },
        method: 'POST',
        // in case of ENTRAID we need to send scope param:
        data: `grant_type=client_credentials&response_type=token&client_id=${clientid}&scope=${clientid}/.default`
    }
    const response = await axios(options)
    return response.data.access_token
}

async function decode(jwt){
    let jwtBase64Encoded = jwt.split('.')[1];
    let jwtDecoded = Buffer.from(jwtBase64Encoded, 'base64').toString('ascii');
    console.log('===> JWT: ' + jwtDecoded);
    let jwtDecodedJson = JSON.parse(jwtDecoded);
    console.log('===> JWT: client_id: ' + jwtDecodedJson.client_id);
    console.log('===> JWT: issuer: ' + jwtDecodedJson.iss);
}


/* SCRIPT */

async function run(){
    // fetch JWT 
    const jwt = await fetchJwtToken(CLIENTID, SECRET, `${URL_TOKEN_FULL}`)
    console.log("Fetched JWT token: " + (jwt != undefined))
    decode(jwt)    
}

run()

 

🍀

entry.title: Getting Started with Edge Integration Cell on AWS: A Setup Guide Using SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
What is Edge Integration Cell?

Edge Integration Cell (EIC), part of the SAP Integration Suite, brings a powerful hybrid integration runtime that bridges the gap between the cloud and your private landscape. It provides the flexibility to seamlessly manage APIs and run integration scenarios within a controlled environment, combining the best of both worlds.

With its hybrid deployment model, EIC makes it easy to design and monitor integration content in the cloud, while still allowing it to be securely deployed and run in a customer managed private environment.

 

Use cases and Advantages of EIC

  • Security or compliance use cases: Sensitive data stays safely within an enterprise's firewall, following strict rules to keep it within a private environment.
  • Migration path for SAP Process Integration customers: EIC helps customers design and monitor integration content in the cloud, while keeping deployment and execution fully within their private environments.

This blog offers a step-by-step guide to deploying Edge Integration Cell on AWS, with two setup options: a quick setup for trying out the simplest setup and a high-availability configuration for production. Whether you're looking for a quick test or a scalable, production-ready deployment, this guide makes it easy to configure SAP BTP and AWS resources. Once the setup is complete, the SAP Integration Suite can deploy integration flows securely to on-premise setup using EIC. 

 

Architecture

Both the quick setup and the high availability (HA) setup architecture for deploying EIC on AWS utilize Amazon Elastic Kubernetes Service (EKS). The quick setup uses the default Virtual Private Cloud (VPC) in the region, with all storage handled by Amazon Elastic Block Store (EBS) and smaller compute and storage resources for a simple and efficient deployment. In contrast, the HA architecture is spread across at least three availability zones for redundancy, with dynamic storage managed by a combination of EBS and Amazon Elastic File System (EFS), along with additional services like Amazon Relational Database Service (RDS) for persistence and Redis for caching.

On the SAP BTP side, the Edge Integration Cell must be activated within the SAP Integration Suite. Once the EIC is configured and successfully deployed on the AWS EKS cluster, integration flows can be designed and seamlessly deployed to the new EIC runtime, combining the powerful integration capabilities of SAP Integration Suite with the customer managed resources on AWS.

 

Edge Integration Cell setup overview on SAP BTP and AWS

Here is an outline of the steps involved in setting up and deploying Edge Integration Cell from SAP BTP environment to AWS.

Setup on AWS:

 

Setup on SAP BTP: 

 

You can find the detailed setup instructions in the following GitHub repository – Deploy SAP Integration Suite - Edge Integration Cell on Amazon Web Services.

 

Additional resources

Here are some helpful resources related to Edge Integration Cell:

  • What is Edge Integration Cell | SAP Help
  • Deploy Edge Integration Cell on Microsoft Azure using Azure Kubernetes Service
  • Deploy Edge Integration Cell on a custom server using SUSE Rancher
  • FAQ on Edge Integration Cell
  • SAP Integration Suite | SAP Help

 

Conclusion

I hope this blog has helped you set up Edge Integration Cell on AWS, making it easier to connect SAP on-premise solutions with the flexibility to design and monitor integrations in the cloud. By following these steps, you can build secure, high-performing business applications that blend innovation with control in a safe environment.

I want to express my gratitude to our SAP team members, Weikun Liu, Sangeetha Krishnamoorthy, Dante Alipio Jr., and Sivakumar N for their valuable contributions, guidance, and support. If you have any questions, feel free to contact paa@sap.com.

entry.title: Integrate SAP LeanIX with Integration Assessment in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Integration Assessment

In the mission, you will start using Integration Assessment for defining, documenting, and executing an integration technology strategy for your organization. After you will have configured Integration Assessment you will process a sample business solution request to specify your integration requirements and an interface request to determine the most suitable integration technology with the help of the intelligent proposal service.
 

SAP LeanIX

Key features of LeanIX are:

  1. Application Portfolio Management: Helps assess and manage the applications in use, facilitating retirement or replacement decisions.
  2. Integration with SAP: Provides tailored functionalities for SAP users, allowing them to leverage existing SAP data and processes.
  3. Collaboration Tools: Supports teamwork and communication among stakeholders involved in enterprise architecture initiatives.
  4. Reporting and Dashboards: Offers visualizations and analytics to track progress and performance metrics.

SAP system integration is a key process for enterprise architects embarking on application modernization. Yet, it isn’t always straightforward.

SAP is the world’s largest provider of enterprise application software, used by 87% of all global commerce and 99 of the world’s 100 largest companies. At the core of SAP’s impressive market share is their ability to deliver value through seamless integration. Yet, to benefit from this, SAP must be connected to other key business systems, which may be in need of re-organization.

The technical and strategic change management considerations related to SAP system integration require thorough preparation. Many companies fail to meet the full potential of integration because of these challenges.

In this guide, we’ll break down seven steps you can take to meet integration challenges head-on and execute the process with confidence.

 

SAP Standard Integration Package by NTT DATA Business Solutions

This integration package is designed to synchronize data between SAP LeanIX and Integration Assessment, focusing primarily on the synchronization of Applications, Vendors and Interface Requests. Utilizing the SAP Integration Suite, this synchronization process ensures a seamless, secure, and up-to-date data flow. This enhances the accuracy and efficiency of IT integration strategies, providing comprehensive insights for integration assessments

Note: Due to the equivalence of 'Vendors' in Integration Assessment to 'Providers' in SAP LeanIX for simplicity and clarity, 'vendor' is used as the uniform term throughout the package.

The package comprises four distinct integrations, each serving unique functions and designed to operate independently. Users can select and deploy any iFlow according to their specific requirements and needs:

  1. Fullsync Integration Assessment Vendors: Synchronizes all vendors (providers) from SAP LeanIX to Integration Assessment. This interface is triggered manually and operates once per trigger.
  2. Fullsync Integration Assessment Applications: Synchronizes all applications from SAP LeanIX to Integration Assessment. This includes optional synchronization of application instances (on-premise/public-cloud/private-cloud). This interface is also manually triggered and operates once per trigger.
  3. Create Integration Assessment Vendor: Activated whenever a factsheet of type 'provider' is created in SAP LeanIX. This event triggers the creation of a new vendor in Integration Assessment, ensuring real-time data updates.
  4. Create Integration Assessment Application: Similar to the vendor creation interface, this is activated when a factsheet of type 'application' is created in SAP LeanIX. It triggers the creation of a new application in Integration Assessment, including the synchronization of application instances (on-premise/public-cloud/private-cloud) if applicable. This interface also requires manual triggering and operates once per trigger.
  5. Create SAP LeanIX Interface Factsheet: Synchroniz a SAP LeanIX Interface Fact Sheet from Integration Assessment Interface Requests at a configurable interval. Applications selected in the Interface Request will also be referenced in the Fact Sheet, provided they have been synced by this package.

https://api.sap.com/package/SAPLeanIXIntegrationwithSAPIntegrationSuite/overview 

User Journey

New created applications in SAP LeanIX

Automated Sync to Integration Assessment

New Business Solution Request in Integration Assessment

New Interface Fact Sheet in SAP LeanIX

Outlook

  • Does that Integration help you?
  • Would you like to integrate with other tools?
  • What are you use cases?

Please use the comment or send me a message 🙂
I'll be happy to discuss your scenario and requirements around SAP Integration Suite and SAP LeanIX!

entry.title: Procedure For End-To-End Encryption And Signing Of File Through SFTP Via Integration Suite PART-1
entry.content:
I have a requirement where I must process a payment file generated in SAP through integration suite by encrypting and signing the file, sending the file to the bank server. We are sending and receiving the payment file through SFTP, to connect to the bank server, we have provided them with a PUBLIC SSH KEY. We were given the public key of the receiver, folder path to place the file, server address and credentials of the same to login and we must process the file generated in SAP to the receiver by encrypting and signing the file. We should encrypt the data using receiver’s public key and sign the same using our private key.

Procedure: -

I am providing a glimpse of usage of the SSH keys and public and private keys of both tenant and receiver below, SSH PRIVATE KEY of the tenant is used to connect the bank server, we share our SSH PUBLIC KEY to the receiver who is bank in our case so, they will map the SSH PUBLIC KEY in their system and provide us a user assigned to the SSH PUBLIC KEY. BANK PUBLIC KEY is provided to encrypt the payment file in CPI/INTEGRATION SUITE, TENANT PUBLIC KEY is given to the bank to verify the signature of the tenant where it is signed by the TENANT’s PRIVATE KEY in CPI/INTEGRATION SUITE. Lastly BANK’s PRIVATE KEY is used to decrypt the file when they receive the file from CPI through SFTP and they will verify the same using TENANT’s PUBLIC KEY. We must add the public key of the receiver and the public key and private key of ours generated through the procedure explained below to PGP keys in monitor -> manage security -> PGP Keys. Since this is a huge procedure, I will be sharing all the information related in two blog posts.

PROCEDURE TO GENERATE AND CONVERT PRIVATE PPK FORMAT KEY TO OPEN SSH KEY PEM FORMAT AND .P12 FORMAT THROUGH PUTTY KEY GENERATOR AND OPENSSL

How to generate a public and private SSH key using putty key generator (which will be used for authentication): -

Above picture shows the main screen of putty key generator

 

Steps to generate the SSH public and private keys: -

There is an option of generate and load at the bottom right of the screen shown above, click on generate and you will be getting below screen,

The above screen shows the generation of the public and private keys, just move the cursor as instructed above and the keys will be generated.

This is the screen showing the creation of the keys, just click on save public key and provide a name and location, the public key is saved, then click on the save private key by providing key passphrase and confirm the same and save, now you have successfully generated public and private keys using a putty key generator, public key is saved in file format and private key is saved in PPK format, but in order to save the keys in SAP CPI we need the file in .p12 format so we need to convert the PPK format file to .p12 format and for that we need OPENSSL.

 Converting the file from PPK to .p12:-

Here is the link to download OPENSSL for your system https://slproweb.com/products/Win32OpenSSL.html, using this download the OPENSSL and place private key which in PPK format in the bin folder of the OPENSSL, open command prompt in windows as an administrator and provide file path to the bin folder as a command, access dir of the bin folder and you should be able to see the private key file in PPK format you pasted in OPENSSL bin folder, https://www.kellton.com/kellton-tech-blog/how-to-generate-import-ssh-keys-in-sap-pi/po is the link with procedure explanation, using the following command openssl req -new -x509 -days 3650 -key <PrivateKey.pem> -out <x509_PrivateKey.pem> ,using the above command, you will be able to convert the file from PPK format to x509 PEM format, using the following command  openssl pkcs12 -export -in <x509_PrivateKey.pem> -inkey <PrivateKey.pem> -out <PrivateKey.p12>, using the above command, you will be able to convert the file from PEM x509 format to .p12 format which can be uploaded to SAP CPI keystore as a keypair.

Procedure for generating key pair (includes both public and private keys):

We need gpg4win to generate keys, we can find the required version to download and install by using the link here  https://files.gpg4win.org/gpg4win-2.3.4.exe, we require 2.3.4 version of gpg4win, after downloading the same please make sure to deselect the Kleopatra and select GPA while installing the components in the package, after installation procedure is completed, please create tenant specific directories and make sure to have three files specified gpa.conf, gpg.conf, run_gpa.bat in the same directory for key management, this procedure of creating tenant specific directories and placing the above files in the directory is mandatory for each tenant separately, find the link to download those file here Creating Tenant-Specific File Directories | SAP Help Portal, adapt the file run_gpa.bat by entering the path to the tenant-specific directory. These files are required to configure the usage of the GPA tool. The file run_gpa.bat sets the shell variable GNUPGHOME to the tenant-specific directory. The files gpa.conf and gpg.conf contain configurations for GPA and GPG. The file gpg.conf, for example, determines the strength of the applied encryption.

After running the GPA tool, you will be seeing a screen like this,

On the top you can find Windows, on clicking it you can find keyring manager, just click it and setup the keyring manager.

After setup you can find the screen like below,

Please go the keys in the top; after clicking the keys you can find an option of new key after clicking the new key you will be getting a popup like this,

Complete the procedure by filling up your name, mail id and passphrase and select the folder path where you want to save the keys, and you are now able to generate the keys. After the keys are generated, please find that the public and private keys both are saved in a single file, you can just copy and paste both in separate files through notepad/notepad++ and just upload the same in the PGP keys in manage security, monitor.

Procedure to consume the KEYS in to the PGP KEYS:

Please upload the keys generated through GPA to the PGP Keys in manage security below,

Go to PGP keys and there you can find an option of add like below,

Please go to add and there you can find public keys and secret keys options, upload public and secret keys separately and later you can observe both the keys will be in one type when you uploaded both public and secret keys, if not it shows only public or secret type in type, please find below reference image,

Till now, we have seen generating SSH keys and public private keys, we will see iflow design part in the next blog post.

 

Please let me know any points/suggestions regarding this blog post, so I will address the same in next blog post. We value your feedback.

entry.title: Use SOAP API with ProcessDirect to call a RFC
entry.content:
In this article, I am going to document how I achieve SOAP based integration. let me give a short background we have more than 30 BAPIs  which are consumed by Non-SAP applications. The simplest way is to create 30 SOAP APIs/iFlows which call request reply and call a RFC adapter in the end.

So, I asked myself, can I do it more smartly, that will help in future as well as minimize my current workload also. Then I figure out two options:

  1. Create an iFlow which can be invoked from another iFlow (SOAP API in this example) and that execute the RFC and give response back to the calling iFlow.
  2. Create a SOAP API Proxy in SAP APIM, against each BAPI and call a standard SOAP API from Cloud Integration which calls the RFC functional module. 

In this blog I am documenting Option 1 but I think Option 2 is more smart, the only issue I find in implementing Option 2 is, I was facing issue to verify the SOAP WSDL definition in proxy. Due to which I opted Option 1 in the end. But to avoid direct access I create proxy for each project in SAP APIM and expose the proxy endpoint to the end user. 

so, let's begin with option 1 today. In my next blog, I document option 2 as well, might it will be helpful to someone.  

Steps 1: Create an iFlow 

  • Create (or Edit) a package named "General"
  • After, create a new iFlow (Artifact type "Integration Flow") named it "Call RFC"
  • Connect Sender with Start event using adapter "ProcessDirect" from the list. Go to settings of ProcessDirect and then set an address in the connection tab (this address will be used later, so copy it in the notepad for later use).

  • Then, add Request-Reply and connect an arrow with Receiver using RFC adapter from the list, and one is already connected with end event (leave it as it is). Open the setting and set the destination of RFC.

  • Save and Deploy the artifact.  

Step 2: Create a SOAP API 

  • Create a new artifact with type API ( choose SOAP API on the next screen) in the existing package or create new one.
  • Remove the existing "Handle Request" integration sub-process along with Process Call  Step. 
  • Configure the SOAP adapter setting 
SOAP Connection Details 
Addressas required (/salesforce/get-customer-details)
Service DefinitionWSDL
URL to WSDLbrowse or select the appropriate WSDL definition

  • Connect end event with receiver and select ProcessDirect as adapter from list. Open the setting and set the address (you have copied in the notepad in step 1). 

  • Save and Deploy the artifact.

Step 3: Test the API

  • Go to monitor > Integrations and APIs and the search the newly deployed SOAP API

  • Download the WSDL definition
  • Open the SoapUI and create a new SOAP Project (go to file menu)
  • Give a proper name and browse the wsdl file you downloaded earlier. 
  • A new request will be created in the project head. 
  • Input the username/password in authorization tab under basic authentication.
  • and submit the request, upon successful connectivity you will get the desired result. 

 

Note: Then I simply create SOAP APIs and update the WSDL definition accordingly.

entry.title: SAP Business AI Testing = SAP API Testing
entry.content:
Introduction - why testing APIs is important for SAP Business AI 

When we talk about SAP Business AI in business we need to remember one thing: AI cannot work without APIs. In fact, testing SAP Business AI is all about testing how well APIs work. Let’s break down why this is so important.

Figure 1 - generative AI cannot do any business activity without SAP APIs 

Only AI powered Chatbots in SAP Business AI? 

SAP Business AI has a potential to eliminate all frontend applications (SAP GUI, Fiori and all other front end applications) and leave only chatbots (Joule, Copilot, etc.) to work with all SAP business functions of SAP S/4HANA. This might mean that all business functions will be exposed via APIs. 

Figure 2 - SAP Gui - sailing away into the past 

Why APIs Are Important for SAP Business AI

APIs (Application Programming Interfaces) are like bridges that let different programs talk to each other. AI, especially advanced AI like generative AI, needs APIs to get information and connect with other systems. Without these connections, AI can't do much.

Integration is the backbone of digital transformations, and APIs are the building blocks for every SAP customer who sells, buys, or transports their products. This means APIs are the key to making all of a company’s systems work together. For AI, APIs are what allow it to be part of that big picture.

How APIs Help SAP Business AI Work

Think of AI like a chef who needs ingredients to make a meal. The API is the helper that brings those ingredients to the kitchen. Without it, the chef (AI) can’t do their job. Here’s how APIs help AI:

  1. Getting Data: AI needs a lot of information to make smart decisions. APIs bring that data from different places to the AI.
  2. Executing Business Activities: To do any business task—like creating an order, delivering goods, or managing inventory—AI needs to use APIs to make things happen.
